content:
# Titus Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 222-223 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/TIT_002_page_001.jpg

### Living Out Sound Teaching

1. But as for you, teach what is consistent with sound doctrine.

2. Older men should be sober-minded, worthy of respect, and self-controlled — solid in their faith, their love, and their endurance.

3. In the same way, older women should live in a way that honors God. They must not be slanderers or slaves to heavy drinking. Instead, they should teach what is good,

4. so that they can train the younger women to love their husbands and their children,

5. to be self-controlled and pure, to manage their households well, to be kind, and to submit to their own husbands, so that no one will be able to speak against God's message.

6. In the same way, encourage the young men to be self-controlled.

7. And you yourself must be an example to them by doing good works of every kind. Let everything you teach be done with integrity and seriousness.

8. Let your message be so sound that it cannot be criticized, so that anyone who opposes you will be put to shame because there is nothing bad to say about us.

9. Slaves must submit to their masters in everything and try to please them. They must not talk back

10. or steal from them. Instead, they must show complete and genuine faithfulness in everything, so that in every way they will make the teaching about God our Savior attractive.

### The Grace That Changes Everything

11. For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people.

12. It trains us to reject godless living and worldly desires, and to live in this present age with self-control, integrity, and devotion to God,

13. while we wait for the blessed hope — the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ.

14. He gave himself for us to set us free from every kind of lawlessness and to purify for himself a people who are his very own, eager to do what is good.

15. Teach these things. Encourage and correct with full authority. Do not let anyone look down on you.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "Sound doctrine" as lived reality:** Paul's word for "sound" is hygiainouse (from which we get "hygiene") — doctrine that is healthy, that produces health. For Paul, theology is never merely intellectual. It is healthy or sick, and you can tell by looking at how people live. Titus 2 is Paul's prescription for a healthy community.

**Verses 2-6 — The household code:** Paul moves through every demographic in the Cretan church — older men, older women, younger women, younger men — and gives each group a portrait of maturity. The common thread binding all groups is sophron (self-controlled, sound-minded). It appears in some form for every group. In a culture known for excess (Paul has just quoted the Cretan proverb about liars and lazy gluttons), self-mastery is revolutionary.

**Verses 3-4 — Older women as teachers:** The word kalodidaskalous (teachers of what is good) is found only here in the New Testament. Paul creates a compound word to describe a distinctive role: older women forming younger women. The church's first seminary was not a building — it was a relationship between generations of women.

**Verse 5 — "So that God's word will not be slandered":** The practical instructions here are not timeless rules dropped from heaven. They are missionary strategy. Paul is concerned that the behavior of Christians in Crete will either commend or discredit the gospel in a watching culture. Every instruction in this chapter carries that same weight: your life is either an argument for the gospel or against it.

**Verses 11-14 — The theological heart of the letter:** After all the practical instructions, Paul reveals the engine that drives them: grace. Not willpower, not cultural conformity — grace. And this grace does two things simultaneously: it saves (verse 11) and it trains (verse 12). The Greek paideuousa means to educate, to discipline, to form — the way a parent raises a child. Grace is not permission to do whatever we want. Grace is the patient teacher who reshapes us from the inside out.

**Verse 13 — "Our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ":** This is one of the most direct affirmations of Christ's divinity in the New Testament. The single Greek article (tou megalou theou kai soteros) binds "God" and "Savior" together as referring to one person: Jesus Christ. The Sinaiticus text is clear and unambiguous here. The hope of the church is not an idea or a program — it is the return of a person who is both God and Savior.

**Verse 14 — "A people who are his very own":** The phrase laon periousion echoes Exodus 19:5 and Deuteronomy 14:2 — Israel as God's treasured possession, his special people. Paul takes the most exclusive language of the Old Testament and applies it to the multi-ethnic church in Crete. The slaves and freedmen, the Cretans and Jews, the old and young — all of them together are now God's treasured people. The boundary has moved from ethnicity to eagerness: "eager to do what is good."

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
